subject: Constitutional Law
lesson_title: Undue Delay and the Right to Expeditious Trial

questions.question: The constitutional right to an expeditious trial — freedom from undue delay — is derived from which provision of the Irish Constitution?

options.B: Art. 38.1 — trial in due course of law
options.C: Art. 34.1 — administration of justice in courts

questions.explanation: The right to an expeditious trial and freedom from undue delay in criminal proceedings forms part of the guarantee of trial 'in due course of law' under Art. 38.1. The Supreme Court recognised in State (Healy) v Donoghue [1976] IR 325 and developed in subsequent case law that this guarantee includes the right to have charges heard without unreasonable delay, since prolonged uncertainty and pending charges inflict significant prejudice on an accused person.

questions.question: In Barker v Wingo (1972) (US), the multi-factor balancing test for undue delay was established. The Irish courts adopted a similar approach in DPP v Byrne [1994] 2 IR 236 (Supreme Court), identifying which key factors?

options.A: Length of delay, reason for the delay, whether the accused asserted the right, and actual prejudice to the accused
options.B: The severity of the offence, the number of prosecution witnesses, court resources, and the accused's criminal record
options.C: The length of delay alone — delay beyond 12 months automatically triggers prohibition
options.D: Only actual prejudice to the defence — systemic delay is not constitutionally relevant

questions.explanation: DPP v Byrne [1994] 2 IR 236 is the foundational Irish authority on undue delay. The Supreme Court (Finlay CJ) identified the following factors: (1) the length of the delay; (2) the reason for the delay (prosecution fault, systemic delay, or accused-caused delay); (3) whether the accused asserted their right to a speedy trial; and (4) actual prejudice caused to the accused (including anxiety, impairment of defence, and loss of memory). No single factor is determinative — the court conducts an overall balancing exercise to determine whether prohibition of the trial is warranted.

questions.question: In the context of undue delay applications, what is the principal remedy sought by an accused and what standard governs its grant?

options.A: A stay of proceedings — granted once any prejudice is demonstrated
options.B: Prohibition of the trial by order of the High Court — granted where the delay is such that a fair trial is no longer possible or would be an abuse of process
options.C: Dismissal of the charge by the trial court — a ministerial function requiring no judicial assessment
options.D: Acquittal — automatically follows from a finding of undue delay

questions.explanation: The primary remedy for undue delay is an order of prohibition from the High Court (by judicial review), restraining the DPP from continuing the prosecution. The threshold for prohibition is high: the delay must be such that the accused's right to a fair trial has been or will be irreparably compromised. In more recent case law (SH v DPP [2006] 3 IR 575), the Supreme Court emphasised that actual prejudice to the defence — not merely the length of delay — is typically the decisive factor. Systemic delay (caused by the court system) may ground a remedy but is assessed differently from prosecutorial delay.

questions.question: In cases of sexual abuse alleged to have occurred many years or decades before prosecution, the Irish courts have held that:

options.A: Prohibition is automatic where the alleged offences occurred more than ten years before prosecution
options.B: Prohibition may be granted where the accused establishes real and serious prejudice to their ability to defend the case by reason of the delay, assessed in light of all the circumstances including the nature of the offences and any explanation for the delay
options.C: Prosecution of historical sexual offences is unconstitutional per se
options.D: No special rules apply — the standard Byrne v DPP balancing test applies without modification

questions.explanation: Historical sexual abuse cases present particular difficulty because the delay is often attributable to the complainant's inability (psychological or practical) to report the offence earlier — a matter outside the prosecution's control. The Supreme Court in PC v DPP [1999] 2 IR 25 and subsequent cases established that in such cases: (a) the court must consider whether the accused has suffered real and serious prejudice as a result of the delay; (b) the accused bears the initial burden of establishing prejudice; and (c) the domination/coercion theory (that the accused's conduct caused the delay) may be relevant. Prohibition is not automatic — each case turns on its facts.

questions.question: Prosecutorial delay — delay caused by the DPP or An Garda Síochána in progressing a prosecution — is treated differently from systemic delay. Which statement correctly distinguishes the two?

options.A: Prosecutorial delay is never a valid ground for prohibition; only systemic delay can ground a remedy
options.B: Prosecutorial delay attributable to fault or lack of reasonable expedition by the prosecution is weighed more heavily against the State; systemic delay (caused by court resources/waiting lists) requires proof of actual prejudice to ground prohibition
options.C: Systemic delay is unconstitutional per se; prosecutorial delay requires proof of bad faith
options.D: Both types of delay are treated identically — the length of delay is the sole criterion regardless of cause

questions.explanation: Irish courts distinguish between prosecutorial delay (where the State fails to act with reasonable expedition) and systemic delay (caused by limited court resources, listing backlogs, etc.). Prosecutorial delay is weighed more heavily against the State because it represents a failure by those responsible for the prosecution to advance it. By contrast, systemic delay — though regrettable — requires the accused to demonstrate that the delay has caused actual prejudice to their defence before prohibition will be granted. This distinction was clarified in Cormack v DPP [2009] 4 IR 397 and related case law.

questions.question: SH v DPP [2006] 3 IR 575 (Supreme Court) represents an important development in undue delay jurisprudence. Its key holding is that:

options.A: Any delay of over five years in a sexual offence case automatically grounds prohibition
options.B: The court should adopt a more nuanced approach to prejudice — the question is whether there is a real risk that due to the delay the accused cannot get a fair trial, assessed at the time of the prohibition application
options.C: In historical sexual abuse cases, the court should always favour prosecution regardless of delay
options.D: Prohibition of a trial on grounds of delay is no longer available as a remedy

questions.explanation: SH v DPP [2006] 3 IR 575 refined the approach to undue delay. The Supreme Court emphasised that the central question is whether, as a result of the delay, there is a real risk that the accused cannot receive a fair trial — not merely whether the accused has suffered some prejudice from the passage of time. The Court also clarified that prejudice should be assessed concretely and not presumed from delay alone, and that the accused bears the burden of demonstrating the real risk of an unfair trial. This decision narrowed the circumstances in which prohibition would be granted and reflected greater deference to the public interest in prosecution.

questions.question: An accused person waited 4 years from charge to trial, primarily due to court listing delays (systemic delay). They allege no specific prejudice to their defence. In light of SH v DPP [2006] 3 IR 575, prohibition is:

options.A: Likely to be granted — any delay of over 3 years in criminal proceedings is presumptively unconstitutional
options.B: Unlikely to be granted — without evidence of actual prejudice to the accused's ability to mount a defence, systemic delay of 4 years, while regrettable, will not automatically ground prohibition
options.C: Certain to be granted — systemic delay caused by State court resources is always attributable to the prosecution
options.D: To be assessed solely by the DPP, not the courts

questions.explanation: Following SH v DPP [2006] 3 IR 575, systemic delay — even of significant duration — does not automatically ground prohibition in the absence of demonstrated actual prejudice to the accused's ability to defend the case. The accused must show a real risk that the delay has compromised their defence (e.g., witnesses unavailable, memory impaired, evidence destroyed). Four years of systemic delay, while far from ideal and potentially raising concerns under Art. 6 ECHR, will not by itself warrant prohibition if the accused can still receive a fair trial.

questions.question: The 'domination theory' or 'dominion theory' in historical sexual abuse delay cases holds that:

options.A: The accused's position of authority over the complainant explains and may excuse the complainant's delay in reporting, so that delay attributable to this dynamic is not held against the prosecution
options.B: The accused must be kept in a position of dominance during the trial process
options.C: The State has a dominant interest in prosecution that outweighs individual rights to an expeditious trial
options.D: Courts may dominate the evidence-gathering process in historical abuse cases

questions.explanation: The domination or dominion theory — recognised in PC v DPP [1999] 2 IR 25 and earlier cases — holds that where the accused held a position of authority, trust, or control over the complainant (e.g., as a parent, teacher, or religious figure), the accused's conduct itself may have caused or contributed to the complainant's delay in reporting. In such cases, the delay is partly attributable to the accused's own behaviour, and it would be inequitable to allow the accused to benefit from that delay by seeking prohibition. Courts factor this dynamic into the overall balancing exercise.

questions.question: Art. 6(1) ECHR guarantees a hearing 'within a reasonable time.' How does this interact with the Irish constitutional right to an expeditious trial?

options.A: Art. 6(1) ECHR replaces the constitutional right — Art. 38.1 no longer applies to delay
options.B: Both Art. 38.1 and Art. 6(1) protect against undue delay; the ECHR standard (incorporated via ECHR Act 2003) reinforces and supplements the constitutional standard, and ECtHR jurisprudence is a relevant — though not binding — guide for Irish courts
options.C: Art. 6(1) ECHR provides greater protection than the Constitution — in all conflict cases, Art. 6(1) prevails
options.D: Art. 6(1) ECHR applies only to civil proceedings, not criminal proceedings in Ireland

questions.explanation: Both the constitutional right (Art. 38.1) and the Convention right (Art. 6(1) — 'hearing within a reasonable time') protect against undue delay. The ECHR Act 2003 incorporates Art. 6(1) at sub-constitutional level; Irish courts must, per s.3(1), perform their functions in a manner compatible with Convention rights. ECtHR case law on 'reasonable time' provides useful guidance on factors such as the complexity of the case, the accused's conduct, and state/judicial conduct, and is considered by Irish courts alongside domestic constitutional analysis. The Constitution remains the primary standard.

questions.question: An accused is charged with offences alleged to have occurred 15 years ago. They seek prohibition, arguing that the delay means they cannot remember their precise movements on the relevant dates. The prosecution argues that the offences were serious and the complainant only recently came forward. How should the court approach this application?

options.A: Grant prohibition automatically — 15 years of delay is always constitutionally unacceptable
options.B: Conduct the balancing exercise in DPP v Byrne [1994]: assess the length and reason for the delay, whether the accused asserted their right, and whether the accused has demonstrated a real risk that the delay has prejudiced their ability to mount a fair defence
options.C: Refuse prohibition — serious offences can never be prohibited on delay grounds
options.D: Grant prohibition — memory impairment is always a sufficient ground

questions.explanation: Even a 15-year delay does not automatically ground prohibition. The court applies the balancing test: (1) length of delay — significant weight; (2) reason — a complainant's delayed reporting (possibly due to trauma, dominion, or psychological factors) is assessed, as is any prosecutorial delay post-report; (3) whether the accused asserted their right; and (4) actual prejudice — the court considers whether general memory impairment alone constitutes 'real and serious' prejudice or whether the accused can identify specific defence evidence that has been lost. The public interest in prosecution of serious offences is weighed against the right to an expeditious trial.

questions.question: Which of the following forms of prejudice is most likely to ground prohibition for undue delay in an Irish criminal trial?

options.A: General anxiety and stress suffered by the accused during the period of delay
options.B: The death of a key alibi witness who could no longer give evidence in the accused's defence, where no other evidence of the accused's whereabouts is available
options.C: The accused's difficulty in recalling where they were on the date of the alleged offence
options.D: Adverse media coverage of the prosecution during the period of delay

questions.explanation: The death of a key alibi witness — where that witness was central to the accused's proposed defence and their evidence cannot be replicated from another source — represents concrete, specific, and demonstrable prejudice to the accused's ability to mount a fair defence. This is precisely the type of 'real and serious prejudice' that Irish courts require following SH v DPP [2006] 3 IR 575. By contrast, general anxiety (while recognised as a relevant factor) is rarely sufficient alone, general memory impairment is considered normal and does not establish specific prejudice, and adverse media coverage does not directly impair the defence case.

questions.question: Where the High Court grants prohibition of a criminal trial on grounds of undue delay, what happens to the DPP's prosecution?

options.A: The DPP can immediately re-charge the accused and begin fresh proceedings
options.B: The prosecution is permanently halted — the DPP is prohibited by court order from continuing or re-instituting the prosecution
options.C: The prosecution is suspended for 12 months to allow the DPP to gather additional evidence
options.D: The accused is entitled to an award of damages against the State as a matter of right

questions.explanation: An order of prohibition from the High Court restrains the DPP from proceeding with — or re-instituting — the relevant prosecution. It is not a conviction or acquittal but effectively ends the case, as re-prosecution would itself be prohibited by the court order. This makes prohibition a powerful remedy and explains why the courts are cautious in granting it — it permanently forecloses prosecution for the specific offences charged. While the accused may also have a cause of action under Art. 40.3 or the State Liability Act for delay-related wrongs, damages are not an automatic consequence of prohibition.

questions.question: The period of delay for the purpose of an undue delay application in criminal proceedings is measured from:

options.A: The date of the alleged offence to the date of the prohibition application
options.B: The date of charge (or arrest, whichever is earlier) to the date of trial or, if prohibition is sought, the date of the prohibition application
options.C: The date the DPP received the Garda file to the date of trial
options.D: The date the complainant made their statement to Gardaí to the date of charge

questions.explanation: The relevant period for measuring delay in criminal proceedings runs from the point at which the accused became formally subject to the criminal process — typically the date of charge or, in some circumstances, arrest — to the date of trial or, where prohibition is sought in advance of trial, to the date of the application. Pre-charge delay (e.g., delay in the Garda investigation) may be relevant to the overall fairness analysis but the constitutional right to an expeditious trial is principally engaged once formal proceedings commence. In historical abuse cases, the entire sequence — from first complaint through charge to trial — is examined.
